name: Irish Cobbler
image_alt: Round white-skinned Irish Cobbler potatoes freshly dug
description: One of the oldest and most popular early American potato varieties — round, white-skinned, with white fluffy flesh and a classic potato flavor. Ready earlier than most varieties, making it the first fresh potato of the season.
history: An American heirloom dating to the late 1800s, Irish Cobbler was the dominant early potato in American markets for decades. Named by a Massachusetts shoemaker who grew it, it became the benchmark for early potato flavor across New England and beyond.
seasonal_quirks: Plant as soon as soil can be worked in spring — Irish Cobbler handles cold soil better than most. Being an early variety it matures before late blight pressure typically arrives, giving it a natural disease advantage in many climates.
harvest_cues: New potatoes available 2–3 weeks after flowering — small and sweet at this stage. Full harvest when vines yellow and die back. Skins slip easily at first; allow to cure so skins set before storage.
variety_notes: Fluffy, dry flesh makes it exceptional for mashing and baking. Not the best choice for potato salad — falls apart when boiled. The early maturity means you can follow it with a fall crop in the same bed.
preservation: Early potatoes don't store as long as late-season types — plan to use within 2–3 months. Cure before storage.
